summary: DeVault walks through his personal aerc email client configuration for reviewing and merging git patches sent via email. He shares specific keybindings for applying patches with git am, pushing changes, and sending templated thank-you replies that include a push summary. The post is a brief, practical tutorial aimed at developers using the email-based patch workflow he advocates for.
key_points: aerc was designed specifically to support the git-send-email patch review workflow
Custom keybindings chain email operations with git commands (pipe to git am, git push, templated replies) for a seamless review-merge-respond cycle
The thanks template auto-generates a push summary so contributors see exactly what was merged
The entire workflow — read, apply, test, push, thank — stays within the email client and terminal
money_quotes: I use aerc as my email client (naturally — I wrote it, after all)
I designed aerc specifically to be productive for this workflow
This blog post will be boring and clerical, feel free to skip it unless it's something you're interested in
This pipes the entire message into git am -3, in the background
I like to reply to the patch, letting the contributor know their work was merged and thanking them for the contribution
key_insight: A well-configured email client can serve as a complete git patch review environment, making the email-based development workflow fast and ergonomic.
